coredata.dc:title: Si-doped carbonized polymer dot as robust hydrophilic coating using for high efficiency antifogging
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Pan, Kaibo
Wei, Xiaoyu
Zhu, Zhicheng
Liu, Chongming
Yang, Bai
coredata.dc:description: Hydrophilic coating can prevent surface from fogging but its application is limited by low mechanical performance. In this study, a hydrophilic coating was prepared by crosslinking the Si-doped carbonized polymer dot (Si-CPD) with 3-glycidyloxypropyltrimethoxysilane (GPTMS) and ethylene oxide (EO). The hydrophilic coating can be used as robust hydrophilic anti-fogging coating. The Si-CPD derived from ethylene diamine tetraacetic acid (EDTA) and aminopropyl oligosiloxanes (APOS) was successfully prepared via one-step hydrothermal method. Then, a resin solution was prepared by mixing Si-CPD, GPTMS and EO. Epoxy group of GPTMS and EO can react with amino group of Si-CPD. Finally, a composite coating with antifogging function can be obtained by simple heating curing. Due to the introduction of hydroxyl which derived from EO, the coating shows excellent antifogging performance. Meanwhile, the presence of inorganic component endows the coating with outstanding mechanical performance. The coating has great potential in related applications, such as optical lenses, mirrors and other transparency substrates.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Coating
Hybrid material
Carbonized polymer dots
Transparent
Antifogging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Effect of EO/GPTMS ratio on hydrophilicity of coatings
Effect of EO/GPTMS ratio on mechanical properties of coatings
Application as anti-fogging protective coating on polymer substrate

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC) under grant no. 22035001.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Schematic representation of the formation process of Si-CPD. (B) TEM image of Si-CPD. (C) High resolution TEM of Si-CPD. (D) Size distribution histograms of Si-CPD. (E) FTIR spectrum of APOS, EDTA and Si-CPD. (F) Liquid-state 29Si NMR of Si-CPD. (G) The full survey of XPS spectra of the Si-CPD.

(A) FTIR spectra of coating resin during maturing process and curing process. (B) Viscosity change of coating resin during maturing process. (C) Film forming ability of the resin by bar coating. (D) Elements mapping of the coating (inset is SEM photograph of the selected area). (E) AFM morphology of the coating.

(A) Water contact angel of coatings. (B) Water absorption of coatings. Antifogging test of coating AFC-1 (C), AFC-4 (E) and AFC-7 (G) in water vapor (60 ℃). Structure schematic diagram of coating AFC-1 (D), AFC-4 (F) and AFC-7 (H).

(A) Representative nanoindentation load − displacement curves of coatings. Elastic modulus (B) and hardness (C) of each AFC for indentation in coatings (the thickness of the test coating is about 200 μm to eliminate the influence by the substrate). (D) Pencil hardness of the coatings. (E) Scratch resistance of coatings (versus to medicinal gauze).

(A) Transmittance spectrum of coatings with about 50 μm thickness. (B) Transmittance of coatings with different thickness. (C) Haze of coatings with different thickness. (D). Yellow index of coatings. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) Adhesion of AFC-1, AFC-4 and AFC-7 coating to the various substrates. (B) Cyclic antifogging test of coating AFC-4 (inset is the photograph of specimen after 10 cycles antifogging test). (C) Photograph of the AFC-4 coated (left) and uncoated PET film (right) after gauze wear test. (D) AFM morphology of the AFC-4 coated (top) and uncoated PET film (bottom) after gauze wear test.
Fabrication route of the antifogging coating.
Comparison of key properties of anti-fogging coatings.

head.ce:title: Si-doped carbonized polymer dot as robust hydrophilic coating using for high efficiency antifogging

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Supramolecular Structure and Materials, College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun, 130012, People’s Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Supramolecular Structure and Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Supramolecular Structure and Materials, College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun, 130012, People’s Republic of China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Jilin University, Changchun, China.

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: The authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fabrication route of the antifogging coating.

Hydrophilic coating can prevent surface from fogging but its application is limited by low mechanical performance. In this study, a hydrophilic coating was prepared by crosslinking the Si-doped carbonized polymer dot (Si-CPD) with 3-glycidyloxypropyltrimethoxysilane (GPTMS) and ethylene oxide (EO). The hydrophilic coating can be used as robust hydrophilic anti-fogging coating. The Si-CPD derived from ethylene diamine tetraacetic acid (EDTA) and aminopropyl oligosiloxanes (APOS) was successfully prepared via one-step hydrothermal method. Then, a resin solution was prepared by mixing Si-CPD, GPTMS and EO. Epoxy group of GPTMS and EO can react with amino group of Si-CPD. Finally, a composite coating with antifogging function can be obtained by simple heating curing. Due to the introduction of hydroxyl which derived from EO, the coating shows excellent antifogging performance. Meanwhile, the presence of inorganic component endows the coating with outstanding mechanical performance. The coating has great potential in related applications, such as optical lenses, mirrors and other transparency substrates.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Fogging is a common phenomenon in daily life, and parameters such as humidity, temperature, and dew-point can affect the formation of fog. For example, optical lens are easily fogging when its surface temperature is lower than ambient temperature, due to water vapor condenses to forming a layer of droplets on surface[1,2]. Fog formation on the surface of optical lens will seriously impair the transmittance of transparent substrates due to the severe light scattering[3–5]. How to prevent fogging from the surface of lens is crucial to maintain high optical precision of instrument in work process[6,7]. What’s more, fogging on glasses or automobile windshields may lead to traffic accident during driving[8]. Therefore, it is significant to prepare a transparent and anti-fogging surface[9–11]. Coated a layer of hydrophilic polymer film on lens surface is an effective approach to prohibit the fogging[12,13]. Water vapor will instantly spread out across the surface and form a uniform water film to avoiding light scattering when it contacts the hydrophilic polymer film. However, hydrophilic polymer films possess a disadvantage of low scratch resistance that hinders their practical application. Therefore, it still remains a challenge to develop robust antifogging coatings for transparent substrates. A compromise is to introduce nanoparticles, such as SiO2 into the polymer matrix[14,15]. However, due to hydrophilic − hydrophobic effect between the inorganic nanoparticles and the polymer, the inorganic nanoparticles always tend to aggregate in the polymer, thus reducing the transmittance of the coating. Moreover, scratch resistance is important for the practical application of antifogging coatings, as good scratch resistance leads to easy cleaning and a long service life.

Carbonized polymer dot (CPD), as a kind of nano sized, zero-dimensional carbonaceous material, has been developed for optical[16,17], photon[18], photo-electricity[19] materials due to their excellent pho-physical properties. CPD is fabricated by organic molecules or polymers through a bottom-up approach and has been recognized as a quasi-spherical core–shell structure[20]. Various functions can be assigned to CPD by doping with heteroatoms during the synthesis of CPD[21,22]. As sub-class of CPD, silane-functionalized CPD (abbreviating to Si-CPD) has drawn much attention due to their self-film forming properties in recent years[23,24]. Si-CPD was synthesized by using amino silane and polyacid as precursors, which has been regarded as a promising material for establishing hybridized organic and inorganic components in nanoscale[25]. Hence, organic–inorganic two-phase region separation can be avoided, ensuring the uniformity of bulk materials. These characteristics make Si-CPD has extensive potential applications in optical limiting glass[23,26], photonic crystal[27,28], solar concentrator[24,29] and so forth. These characteristics indicate the Si-CPD has a great potential in antifogging and scratch-resistant coating application, but there is lack of relevant researches.

In this study, a simple strategy of preparing robust antifogging coating film is proposed. Firstly, the Si-CPD derived from ethylene diamine tetraacetic acid (EDTA) and aminopropyl oligosiloxanes (APOS) was successfully prepared via one-step hydrothermal method. Secondly, a resin solution was prepared by mixing of Si-CPD, ethylene oxide (EO) and 3-glycidyloxypropyltrimethoxysilane (GPTMS). Thirdly, a composite coating with antifogging function can be obtained by simple heating curing. Due to the introduction of hydroxyl which derived from EO, the coating shows excellent antifogging performance. Meanwhile, the presence of inorganic component makes coating possess outstanding mechanical performance. Such a scratch- resistant antifogging coating based on Si-CPD was prepared firstly and has great potential in related applications, such as in optical lenses, mirrors and other transparency substrates.

(3-aminopropyl)triethoxysilane (APTES, 99 %), tetraethylammonium hydroxide (25 wt% in water), ethylene oxide (EO, 99.5 %), 3-glycidyloxypropyltrimethoxysilane (GPTMS, 97 %), ethylene diamine tetraacetic acid (EDTA, 99.5 %) and glycidyl methacrylate (GMA, 97 %, with 100 ppm stabilizer MEHQ) were purchased from Aladdin. Isopropyl alcohol (99.7 %), propyl alcohol (＞99 %), acetonitrile (＞99 %) were purchased from Beijing Chemical Works. All reagents were directly used as received without further purification.

The Si-CPD was synthesized through hydrothermal method. The specific process shows as follow. Firstly, APOS was obtained by based-catalyzed pre-hydrolytic sol–gel reaction using APTES according to our previous work[30]. Briefly, APTES (4.43 g, 0.02 mol) was dissolve in deionized water (1.8 mL), propyl alcohol (0.8 mL), acetonitrile (0.4 mL) and tetraethylammonium hydroxide (0.04 mL) mixture solution and then stirred at 50 ℃ for 24 h. After the pre-hydrolytic sol − gel reaction, the white solid product APOS is obtained by rotating evaporation to remove the solvent and catalyst in the solution. Secondly, EDTA (1.0959 g, 0.00375 mol) and APOS were dissolved in 20 mL deionized water to form mixture solution, which was transferred to a poly(tetrafluoroethylene) autoclave and heated in an oven at 180 °C for 5 h. Thirdly, the large particles are removed using a 0.22 μm polyethersulfone membrane to obtain the pale yellow Si-CPD solution. The Si-CPD was lyophilized for further use.

Dry Si-CPD powder (1 g) was dissolved in 10 mL deionized water, and then blended with a mixture of isopropyl alcohol (10 mL), GPTMS (0.5237 g, 2.2150 mmol) and EO (0.0976 g, 2.2150 mmol) to obtain coating resin. The resin can be used after maturing for 12 ∼ 24 h. The resin was bar-coated and then placed in 60℃ oven for 30 min to evaporating the water. And then, temperature rises to 120℃ for further curing 2 h to obtain coating.
The morphology and size distribution of Si-CPD was recorded by transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM-2100F).
Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was carried out on Bruker VERTEX 80 V.
X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) was investigated by using ESCALAB 250 spectrometer with a mono X-Ray source Al Kα excitation (1486.6 eV).

Liquid-state 29Si NMR of Si-CPD was determined by NMR spectrometer (Bruker, model AV600). Si-CPD was dissolved in D2O. The degree of condensation (DOC) for analysis was calculated according to Equation (1).[31] For convenience, ‘T n ’ is used to describe Si-CPD according to traditional notation. ‘T’ indicates a silicon atom with three functional groups for the hydrolysis and condensation, ‘n’ is the number of silicon-bridging oxygen atoms (n = 0–3).
(1)
D O C
%

T

+

T

T

× 100 %
Viscosity of the resin was performed by rotational rheometer (Waters DHR-2).
Surface morphology and element mapping were recorded by scanning electron microscope (SEM, HITACHI SU8020.) equipped with energy dispersive X-ray (EDX, Bruker Quantax 400).
The roughness of the coating surface was examined using atomic force microscopy (AFM, Bruker ICON-XR).
Thickness of coating was measured by probe step profiler (Vecco DEKTAK 150).

The amount of epoxy reacted with per gram Si-CPD was determined by back titration. Si-CPD (0.1 ∼ 0.3 g) dissolve in water (10 mL) and acetone (5 mL) mixed solvent. Then GMA (0.5 g) add into above mixed solution. In order to making amino of Si-CPD fully reacted with epoxy of GMA, the reaction was stirred at room temperature for 5 h. The residual epoxy of GMA was reacted HCl by adding 20 mL hydrochloric acid-acetone solution. Finally, residual HCl was titration by sodium hydroxide standard solution. The amount of epoxy reacted with per gram Si-CPD was calculated according to Equation (2). C : the amount of epoxy reacted with per gram Si-CPD, mol/g;
m 1
: mass of GMA, g;
m 2
: mass of Si-CPD, g;
c e
: The amount of epoxy of per gram GMA, mol/g;
c 1
: concentration of hydrochloric acid-acetone solution, mol/L;
c 2
: concentration of sodium hydroxide standard solution, mol/L;
V 1
: volume of hydrochloric acid-acetone solution, L;
V 2
: volume of sodium hydroxide standard solution, L.
(2)
C =
m 1
c e

(
c 1
V 1

c 2
V 2
)
m 2
The water contact angles of the coatings were measured using contact angle system OCA (Dataphysics) at ambient temperature.

This work employed the Agilent G200 nanoindentation instrument equipped with Berkovich diamond tips to measure the nanoindentation of coatings. The experimental procedure comprises three stages: first, loading from 0 to 200 mN (+20 mN/s), followed by maintaining a maximum load of 200 mN for 0.5 s, and finally, unloading from 200 to 0 mN (−20 mN/s). In order to eliminate any influence from the substrate, all coating samples used in the nano-indentation tests were coated on glass sheets with a coating thickness of approximately 200 μm.

The pencil hardness test of the coating is based on the Chinese standard GB/T 6739 − 2006 test with a load weight of 1000 g. The brand of pencil was Mitsubishi. The hardness of the pencil ranged from soft to hard varying from 6B to 9H. The damage to the coating surface caused by the pencil was observed by an optical microscopy.
Thermal gravimetric analyzer (TGA) was performed on TA TGA-Q500 under nitrogen atmosphere from room temperature to 800℃ (heating rate 10 °C/min).
Transmittance spectra were measured by a Shimadzu 3100 UV − vis spectrophotometer.
The wear resistance was measured by a gauze resistance tester (Dongguan Neutral Instrument Technology), the test load was 200 g.

Water absorption ratio was determined in a 60℃ temperature and 90 %RH humidity environment. Water absorption ratio was calculated according to Equation (3).
R a
: Water absorption ratio, wt%;
m 1
: Mass of specimen before test, g;
m 2
: Mass of specimen after test, g.
(3)
R a
=
m 2
-
m 1
m 1
× 100 %

The anti-fogging performance of coating was assessed by hot-vapor testing according to Chinese national standard GB/T 31726–2015. The specimen was place on a constant water bath with 60℃, the distance between the coating surface and water surface was 5 cm. The anti-fogging time was recorded start from the placing moment and end by formation of fog.

The fabrication route of the antifogging coating is shown as Scheme 1 . The coating resin is made up of Si-CPD, EO and GPTMS. After maturing process, it can be directly coated on substrate to form antifogging coating by thermocuring. Fig. 1
A represents the formation process of Si-CPD using APOS and EDTA as precursors. APOS was synthesized using (3-aminopropyl)-triethoxysilane (APTES) via base-catalyzed prehydrolytic sol − gel reaction. APOS is hybrid including amino siloxane oligomers with different molecular weights. Its structure has been characterized in our previous work[30]. Herein, cyclic APOS with the largest proportion of –Si-O- units (four) was chosen to elaborate the formation process of Si-CPD. The reaction was conducted by the cross-linking of APOS and EDTA to forming polyamide oligomer and further condensed to Si-CPD during the hydrothermal process. The ratio of –NH2 in APOS and –COOH in EDTA can influence the formation of Si-CPD (Figure S1). EDTA plays a role as like cross-linking agent that bonds the amino siloxane oligomers molecule fragment together. As the ratio of EDTA increases, yellow index and molecular weight of the obtained Si-CPD increase (Fig. S1A, B and D). Excessive –COOH may yield chromophore during hydrothermal process through decarboxylation reaction, leading to higher yellow index[32,33]. The high yellow index is harmful to the chromaticity of light, so that the Si-CPD with low yellow index are better to use for fabricating coating. On the one hand, amino group on the surface of Si-CPD plays a role as cross-linking site to react with epoxy group. On the other hand, amino groups act as base catalysis that conducts the −Si(OMe)3 group of GPTMS hydrolyze to –Si(OH)3 and final to forming Si-O-Si cross-linking network. High ratio of amino groups will result in high pH (Fig. S1C) and will accelerate the rate of sol–gel reaction, leading to a short storage life. Based on above phenomenon, the Si-CPD synthesized from APOS and EDTA precursors with –NH2: –COOH ratio of 1:0.75 was chosen to prepare the coatings. As shown in Fig. 1 B-D, the morphology of Si-CPD exhibits dot shape observed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The average diameter is about 3.75 nm with counted 100Si-CPD particles. Its core structure shows well-resolved lattice with spacing of 0.21 nm which is close to the d-spacing of the graphene (100) planes observed by high resolution TEM. The functional groups of Si-CPD were determined by Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 1 E, broad absorption peaks around 3650 − 3220 cm−1 can be attributed to ν(O H) and ν(N H). The ν(CO) characteristic peak of at 1652 cm−1 shifts to 1632 cm−1 due to the carboxyl transforms into acylamino group. The dual adjacent narrow peaks locating at 1135 and 1030 cm−1 are the typical characteristic peaks of cyclic-like silsequioxane framework[34]. The absorption peak at 929 cm−1 is attributed to ν(Si − O H), indicating the surface of Si-CPD possess unreacted silanol. Furthermore, 29Si NMR could be employed to further ascertain the chemical environment of Si at molecular scale. As shown in Fig. 1 F, the Si-CPD possesses three species of Si: T1 (−48.0 ∼ -51.6 ppm), T2 (−55.9 ∼ -60.8 ppm), T3 (−63.3 ∼ -70.5 ppm). The degree of condensation (DOC) of the Si-CPD is calculated as 66.83 % according to Equation (1), accounting for the unreacted silanol (Si − OH)[35,36]. X-photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis can further support the FTIR results. As shown in Fig. 1 G, Si-CPD mainly consist of O (16.87 at.%), N (5.03 at.%), C (67.85 at.%), Si (10.25 at.%). In high-resolution XPS spectra (Figure S2), the O1s band contains three peaks at 532.59 eV (OH), 531.97 eV (OC) and 531.36 eV (O-Si)[37]. The N1s band can be divided into two peaks at 401.08 eV (NH) and 399.33 eV (NC)[38]. The C1s band can be divided into four peaks at 287.9 eV (CO), 286.33 eV (CO), 285.12 eV (CN), and 284.53 eV (CC)[39,40]. The Si2p band contains two peaks at 102.71 eV (Si-O) and 101.77 eV (Si-C)[41,42].

The amount of epoxy group reacted with per gram of Si-CPD was determined by back titration method using GMA as standardizing reagent (Figure S3). The amount of epoxy group reacted with per gram of Si-CPD was calculated as 4.430 mmol according to Equation S1 (Table S1). Next, the coating resin solution was obtained by mixing Si-CPD (1 g) with 2.215 mmol of EO and 2.215 mmol of GPTMS and the resin can be used after maturing. The reaction of resin during maturing period was monitored by FTIR spectra. As shown in Fig. 2
A, it can be observed primary amine characteristic peak ν(N H, 1585 cm−1) at 0 h, and its intensity decreases gradually during maturing period. It means the amino group reacts with epoxy group. The intensity of primary amine characteristic peak decreases obviously after heat curing. The intensity of characteristic peak around 1033 cm−1 first decreases during maturing period and then increase after heat curing, which may attribute to Si-OMe hydrolyze to Si-OH, and Si-OH can transform into Si-O-Si by heat curing.

As shown in Fig. 2 B, the viscosity of the coating resin solution gradually increases due to sol–gel hydrolysis reaction during maturing period. As maturing time reaches 12 h, it is beneficial to form coating, so the resin can be directly bar-coated on polymer substrate, for instance PET film (Fig. 2 C). Energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectroscopy proves that elements C, O, N, and Si are homogeneously distributed in the coating (Fig. 2 D). The coating shows smooth surface without any cracks (Figure S4), the roughness shown by atomic force microscopy (AFM) is about 0.53 nm (Fig. 2 E).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of EO/GPTMS ratio on hydrophilicity of coatings
ce:section.ce:para:
The hydrophilicity of coating is dependent on the ratio of EO/GPTMS ratio. Here, various coating resin with different EO/GPTMS ratio includes 5:0 (AFC-1), 4:1 (AFC-2), 3:2 (AFC-3), 1:1 (AFC-4), 2:3 (AFC-5), 1:4 (AFC-6) and 0:5 (AFC-7). As shown in Fig. 3
A, the water contact angle increases gradually from AFC-1 to AFC-7. To determine the water absorption of the coatings, all tested specimens were placed in 90 % humidity environment. Fig. 3 B shows the plot of water absorption versus time of the selected coatings AFC-1, AFC-4 and AFC-7. The AFC-1 shows a high water absorption of 19.68 %, and its equilibration time is about 200 min. The water absorption of AFC-4 and AFC-7 is 8.74 % and 3.41 %, respectively, which decreases with the increase of GPTMS. The AFC-1 shows excellent antifogging performance, there has not been observed any fog formation on the coating surface even though putting is directly on the 60 ℃ water vapor over 600 s (Fig. 3 C). It is because the presence of abundant hydroxyl group that endows coating with excellent antifogging ability (Fig. 3 D). As GPTMS proportion increases to EO: GPTMS = 1: 1, the antifogging performance decreases, antifogging time of AFC-4 is 52 s (Fig. 3 E, F). When the GPTMS proportion further increases to EO: GPTMS = 0: 5, the coating AFC-7 losts its antifogging ability due to the absence of hydroxyl group. Once it touched hot vapor, the surface formed fog immediately (Fig. 3 G, H). Other coating’s antifogging time is list in Table S2. Therefore, increasing the EO proportion is beneficial to improving the antifogging ability of the coating.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of EO/GPTMS ratio on mechanical properties of coatings
ce:section.ce:para:
In order to investigate the mechanical properties of the coatings, nanoindentation test was used in this research to measure their modulus and hardness. Fig. 4
A shows the load − displacement curves of coatings. Besides, coating film with low elastic recovery will penetrate deeper under the same loading condition for comparison. Meanwhile, as increase GPTMS proportion, the coating could also be observed hysteresis of the load − displacement curves, corresponding to higher elastic recovery. Furthermore, as shown in Fig. 4 B and C, modulus and hardness of coatings are calculated based on the Oliver-Pharr method[43]. EO is transformed into hydroxyl group grafted on the surface of Si-CPD. Although AFC-1 shows excellent antifogging ability, its mechanical property is unsatisfactory. The mechanical property of coating increases along with GPTMS proportion. The addition of GPTMS will increase the cross-linking degree and the content of inorganic SiOx. But it also sacrifices the anti-fog performance of the coating. For visualization into the surface hardness, pencil hardness test method was performed according to Chinese standard GB/T 6739 − 2006. As shown in Fig. 4 D, the pencil hardness of AFC coating was varied from 2B to 9H with increasing of GPTMS proportion. As shown in Fig. 4 E, the coating with good mechanical properties has better scratch resistance.

Fig. 5
A is the transmittance spectra of the coatings, all coatings with about 50 μm thickness exhibit excellent transmittance in visible light region range from 400 to 800 nm. Fig. 5 B shows that transmittance at 550 nm can maintain above 95 % as the coating thickness increases from 10 to 100 μm. Fig. 5 C shows that the haze of coating can maintain below 3 % as the coating thickness increases from 10 to 50 μm. As shown in Fig. 5 D, all samples show low yellow index (YI) values[44], indicating it can be used as optical coatings. The high transmittance and low haze of AFC can be ascribed to the fact that the organic and the inorganic phases are hybridized in nanoscale Si-CPD, avoiding the light scattering caused by phase separation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application as anti-fogging protective coating on polymer substrate
ce:section.ce:para:
Adhesion is essential property for the application of coating. Here, the adhesion strength of selected specimens (AFC-1, AFC-4 and AFC-7) to various substrates was measured by the pull-off method according to Chinese standard GB/T 5210-2006C. As shown in Fig. 6
A, the adhesion of the coating to glass is extremely higher than polymer substrate due to the formation of covalent bond Si-O-Si between interfaces. The adhesion of the coating to different polymer substrates is relevant to the polarity of polymer material. From AFC-1 to AFC-7, the adhesion strength of coating increases as the proportion of GPTMS. The addition of GPTMS can enhance the crosslinking degree that endows coating with stronger adhesion strength. Although AFC-7 shows outstanding adhesion strength, it doesn’t possess anti-fogging performance. The AFC-4 coating shows adhesion strength to PET and PC polymer substrates with force between 1.51 ∼ 1.87 MPa, which could satisfy the application requirement. Furthermore, AFC-4 was chosen to coat on glasses to test its cyclic antifogging performance. As shown in Fig. 6 B, compared with a bare lens, the AFC-4 coated lens can effectively avoid the formation of fog even after 10 times. To evaluate the scratch resistance of AFC-4 coating, the gauze was chosen for cyclic rubbing against surface of coating. As shown in Fig. 6 C-D, the AFC-4 coating can provide a good protection to polymer film (PET) and there is no scratch on the coated PET film (left). However, the uncoated PET film (right) shows serious scratch resulting in blurry vision. Here, comparison of key properties of some anti-fogging coatings is shown in Table 1 .

In this study, a new design strategy for hydrophilic coating with high surface hardness was developed. Based on nano hybrid approach, the special core − shell nanostructure Si-CPD containing rich functional groups was combined with EO and GPTMS to prepare robust anti-fogging coating. By using a simple heating curing treatment, the coating composed of polar groups and hard silica component can be easily prepared through Si-OH bond crosslinking. Due to the inorganic phase and organic phase are hybridized in nano scale, avoid phase separation which keep excellent transparency. The polar hydroxyl groups mainly derived from EO could can provide outstanding hydrophilic for coating. The presence of inorganic component makes coating possess excellent surface hardness. The hydrophilic and mechanical performance can be adjusted by molar ratio of EO and GPTMS. Increasing EO proportion can improve the hydrophilic and antifogging performance of coating. Increasing GPTMS proportion can enhance the surface hardness of coating. In consideration of both anti-fogging and scratch resistance, the ratio of EO and GPTMS is set as 1: 1, and the corresponding coating exhibits 5H pencil hardness to stand gauze wear test and excellent anti-fogging, simultaneously. The coating has great potential in related applications, such as optical lenses, mirrors and other transparency substrates.

Kaibo Pan: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Investigation. Xiaoyu Wei: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Investigation. Zhicheng Zhu: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis. Chongming Liu: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis. Bai Yang: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC) under grant no. 22035001.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2024.06.012.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A facile antifogging/frost-resistant coating with self-healing ability

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W. Wang, P. Lu, Y. Fan, L. Tian, S. Niu, J. Zhao, L. Ren, A facile antifogging/frost-resistant coating with self-healing ability, Chemical Engineering Journal, 378 (2019) 122173.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Effective antifogging coating from hydrophilic/hydrophobic polymer heteronetwork

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Shi, L. Xu, D. Qiu, Effective Antifogging Coating from Hydrophilic/Hydrophobic Polymer Heteronetwork, Advanced Science, 9 (2022).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The dry-style antifogging properties of mosquito compound eyes and artificial analogues prepared by soft lithography

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Gao, X. Yan, X. Yao, L. Xu, K. Zhang, J. Zhang, B. Yang, L. Jiang, The Dry-Style Antifogging Properties of Mosquito Compound Eyes and Artificial Analogues Prepared by Soft Lithography, Advanced Materials, 19 (2007) 2213-2217.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Water drop-surface interactions as the basis for the design of anti-fogging surfaces: Theory, practice, and applications trends

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: I.R. Durán, G. Laroche, Water drop-surface interactions as the basis for the design of anti-fogging surfaces: Theory, practice, and applications trends, Advances in Colloid and Interface Science, 263 (2019) 68-94.
J. Zhao, L. Ma, W. Millians, T. Wu, W. Ming, Dual-Functional Antifogging/Antimicrobial Polymer Coating, ACS Applied Materials & Interfaces, 8 (2016) 8737-8742.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ototoxic effect of ultrastop antifog solution applied to the guinea pig middle ear

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Nomura, H. Oshima, D. Yamauchi, H. Hidaka, T. Kawase, Y. Katori, Ototoxic Effect of Ultrastop Antifog Solution Applied to the Guinea Pig Middle Ear, Otolaryngology–Head and Neck Surgery, 151 (2014) 840-844.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Knauth, M. Weiss, M. Dave, A. Frotzler, T. Haas, Vergleich von Antibeschlagmethoden in der Endoskopie, Der Anaesthesist, 61 (2012) 1036-1044.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Ke, C. Zhang, H. Chen, Y. Jiang, Robust superhydrophilic antifogging coatings by a facile sol–gel method, Journal of Coatings Technology and Research, 20 (2023) 1343-1352.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Tzianou, G. Thomopoulos, N. Vourdas, K. Ellinas, E. Gogolides, Tailoring Wetting Properties at Extremes States to Obtain Antifogging Functionality, Advanced Functional Materials, 31 (2020) 2006687.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Zwitter-wettability and antifogging coatings with frost-resisting capabilities

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Lee, M.L. Alcaraz, M.F. Rubner, R.E. Cohen, Zwitter-Wettability and Antifogging Coatings with Frost-Resisting Capabilities, ACS Nano, 7 (2013) 2172-2185.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Bai, X. Li, Y. Zhao, L. Ren, X. Yuan, Antifogging/Antibacterial Coatings Constructed by N-Hydroxyethylacrylamide and Quaternary Ammonium-Containing Copolymers, ACS Applied Materials & Interfaces, 12 (2020) 12305-12316.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F. Xu, X. Li, Y. Li, J. Sun, Oil-Repellent Antifogging Films with Water-Enabled Functional and Structural Healing Ability, ACS Applied Materials & Interfaces, 9 (2017) 27955-27963.
